concepts.title: Summation of both finite and infinite number of terms of any Arithmetic-Geometric Series
concepts.conceptBody:
Arithmetic Geometric Series 

Consider the series:

${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=1+2\text{r}+3{{\text{r}}^{2}}+4{{\text{r}}^{3}}+\ldots \ldots +\text{nr}{{\text{r}}^{\left( \text{n}-1 \right)}}$

This series is neither arithmetic (the differences between the terms isn't constant) nor geometric (the ratio of successive terms isn't constant), yet it seems to be something of both.

It looks like something that is familiar yet alien.

If we know :

$\frac{1}{{{(1-r)}^{2}}}={{(1-r)}^{\left( -2 \right)}}=1+2r+3{{r}^{2}}=4{{r}^{3}}+\ldots .+n{{r}^{\left( n-1 \right)}}+\ldots $

then we know the sum to infinity of the series is ${{(1-r)}^{-2}}$, if $\left| r \right|<1$ so the series converges. However, this doesn't tell us the sum to $\text{n}$ terms.

Consider

${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=1+2\text{r}+3{{\text{r}}^{2}}+4{{\text{r}}^{3}}+\ldots ..+\text{nr}{{\text{r}}^{\left( \text{n}-1 \right)}}$ (i)

And using our trick from the geometric series, multiply this by $\text{r}$ :

$r {{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\text{r}+2{{\text{r}}^{2}}+3{{\text{r}}^{3}}+4{{\text{r}}^{4}}+\ldots \ldots +\text{n}{{\text{r}}^{\text{n}}}$ (ii)

Subtract Equations (i) & (ii) :

${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}\left( 1-\text{r} \right)=1+\text{r}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}+\ldots .+{{\text{r}}^{\text{n}-1}}-\text{n}{{\text{r}}^{\text{n}}}$

Nothing we know the formula for the geometric series, and using it :

${{S}_{n}}\left( 1-r \right)=\frac{\left( 1-{{r}^{n}} \right)}{\left( 1-r \right)}-n{{r}^{n}}$

Bringing it all together under one denominator :

${{S}_{n}}\left( 1-r \right)=\frac{\left( 1-{{r}^{n}}-n{{r}^{n}}+n{{r}^{\left( n+1 \right)}} \right)}{\left( 1-r \right)}$

Rounding up like terms, gives us the formula :

${{S}_{n}}=\frac{\left( 1+n{{r}^{\left( n+1 \right)}}-\left( n+1 \right){{r}^{n}} \right)}{\left( 1-{{r}^{2}} \right)}$

Therefore :

$1+2r+3{{r}^{2}}\ldots n{{r}^{\left( n-1 \right)}}=\frac{\left( 1-\left( n+1 \right){{r}^{n}}+n{{r}^{\left( n+1 \right)}} \right)}{{{(1-r)}^{2}}}$

If we multiply throughout by a constant, a we get :

$a+2ar+3a{{r}^{2}}\ldots na{{r}^{\left( n-1 \right)}}=\frac{\left( 1-\left( n+1 \right){{r}^{n}}+n{{r}^{\left( n+1 \right)}} \right)}{{{(1-r)}^{2}}}$

The sum to infinity of this series, when $\text{n}$ tends to infinity (and $\left| \text{r} \right|<1$, is :

$\frac{1}{{{(1-r)}^{2}}}=1+2r+3{{r}^{2}}+4{{r}^{3}}+\ldots .+n{{r}^{\left( n-1 \right)}}+\ldots $

General Arithmetic Geometric Series 

In this series, which is neither geometric nor arithmetic, has the form :

$a+\left( a+d \right)r+\left( a+2d \right){{r}^{2}}+\left( a+3d \right){{r}^{3}}+\ldots .+\left( a+\left( n-1 \right)d \right)r\left( n-1 \right)$

The simple arithmetic-geometric series is a special case of this, where $a=1$

If we expand this series, we get :

$\left( \text{a}+\text{ar}+\ldots \ldots +a{{\text{r}}^{\left( \text{n}-1 \right)}}+\left( \text{dr}\left( 1+2\text{r}+3{{\text{r}}^{2}}\ldots \ldots +\left( \text{n}-1 \right){{\text{r}}^{\left( \text{n}-2 \right)}} \right) \right. \right.$

Naturally, we note the first bit is a normal geometric series, and the second bit is our simple arithmetic geometric series.

That is :

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & a+\left( a+d \right)r+\left( a+2d \right){{r}^{2}}+\left( a+3d \right){{r}^{3}}+\ldots +\left( a+\left( n-1 \right)dr\left( n-1 \right)d{{r}^{\left( n-1 \right)}} \right. \\ {} & ~=a\frac{\left( 1-{{r}^{n}} \right)}{\left( 1-r \right)}+rd\frac{\left. {{(1-nr)}^{\left( n-1 \right)}}+\left( n-1 \right){{r}^{n}} \right)}{{{(1-r)}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

Sum of infinite arithmetic - Geometric sequence 

for $\left| \text{r} \right|<\mid \And \text{n}\to \infty ,{{\text{r}}^{\text{n}}}$ approaches $0$

putting these values in general term obtained above.

$\text{S}\infty =\frac{\text{a}}{1-\text{r}}+\frac{\text{dr}}{{{(1-\text{r})}^{2}}}$
